question_html:
Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones that represent enormous structural diversity in terms of the arrangement of atoms in space, resulting in hundreds of stereoisomers. Although the chemical properties of most stereoisomers may not be very different, their metabolic rate and utilization in biological systems is significantly different and known to influence the overall carbohydrate metabolism. Structural variants, which arise due to a different arrangement of atoms in three-dimensional space are known as stereoisomers. The number of stereoisomers can be theoretically estimated by using the formula 2 n, where '{tex}n{/tex}' is the number of stereocenters or asymmetric (chiral) carbon atoms in a molecule. Out of these stereoisomers, there are some structures, which are mirror images of each other, and they are referred to as enantiomers.

  1. Give chemical reactions to show the presence of an aldehydic group and straight chain in glucose. (2)
    1. Define anomers. (1)

      OR

    2. Draw the structure of {tex}\beta{/tex}-D-Glucopyranose. (1)
  3. Sucrose is known as invert sugar. Explain. (1)

question_html:
Reduce carbohydrates, increase protein to check diabetes, older people may need greater carbohydrates reduction. An average Indian derives {tex}61-64 \%{/tex} of energy from consumption of food rich in carbohydrates. A study published in the Journal of Diabetes Care has recommended reducing this to {tex}49-56 \%{/tex} for remission or prevention of Type 2 diabetes, one of the leading cause of death worldwide. Along with reducing carbohydrate intake, the study suggests that one should also increase protein intake up to {tex}14-20 \%{/tex} of the total energy consumption. Fat should contribute not more than {tex}21-27 \%{/tex} of the total energy consumption. In simple words, {tex}50 \%{/tex} of the plate should consist of fruits and green vegetables, 25% carbohydrates, {tex}25 \%{/tex} food rich in protein. question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Carbohydrates are essential for life in both plants and animals. Carbohydrates are used as storage molecules as starch in plants and glycogen in animals. Chemically they are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones. On the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis, carbohydrates are classified as monosaccharides, oligosaccharides and polysaccharides. All monosaccharides are reducing sugars, i.e., they are oxidized by Tollens' reagent and Fehling's solution. A monosaccharide like glucose is aldohexose and its molecular formula was found to be C6H12O6. After reacting with different reagents like HI, H2N - OH, Bromine water, (CH3CO)2O, etc. its structure was found to contain one aldehyde group, one primary alcoholic group, (-CH2OH) and four secondary alcoholic
groups (> CHOH). Despite having the aldehyde group, glucose does not give some of the reactions of aldehyde group like Schiff's test, NaHSO3 addition. This explains the existence of glucose in two cyclic hemiacetal forms which differ only in the configuration of the hydroxyl group at C-1.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What are reducing sugars? (1)
  2. Classify the following into monosaccharide and disaccharide:
    Fructose, Sucrose, Lactose, Galactose (1)
  3. Name the polysaccharide which is known as 'animal starch'. Why is it called 'animal starch'? (2)

    OR

  1. Name the isomers of glucose which in the cyclic form differ only in the configuration of the -OH group at C-1.
  2. Presence of which functional group was detected when glucose reacted with Br2 water? (2{tex}\times{/tex}1 = 2)

Proteins are the most abundant biomolecules of the living system. Proteins are the polymers of about twenty different {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids which are linked by peptide bonds. Ten amino acids are called essential amino acids. In zwitter ionic form, amino acids show amphoteric behaviour as they react both with acids and bases.
On the basis of their molecular shape, proteins are classified into two types: Fibrous and Globular proteins. Structure and shape of proteins can be studied at four different levels i.e., primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary, each level being more complex than the previous one. The secondary or tertiary structure of proteins get disturbed on change of pH or temperature and they are not able to perform their functions. This is called denaturation of proteins.

Certain organic compounds are required in small amounts in our diet but their deficiency causes specific disease. These compounds are called vitamins. Most of the vitamins cannot be synthesized in our body but plants can synthesize almost all of them. So they are considered as essential food factors. However, the bacteria of the gut can produce some of the vitamins required by us. All the vitamins are generally available in our diet. The term 'vitamin' was coined from the words vital + amine, since the earlier identified compounds had amino group. Vitamins are classified into two groups depending upon their solubility in water or fat namely-fat soluble vitamins and water soluble vitamins.

question_html:
Give reasons for any 3 of the following observations:

  1. Penta-acetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine.
  2. Amino acids behave like salts.
  3. Water soluble vitamins must be taken regularly in diet.
  4. The two strands in DNA are complimentary to each other.
question_text: Give reasons for any 3 of the following observations: Penta-acetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine. Amino acids behave like salts. Water soluble vitamins must be taken regularly in diet. The two strands in DNA are complimentary to each other.
explanation: Penta-acetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine (-OHNH 2 ) because there is no free -CHO group present. Instead of being simple amines or carboxylic acids, amino acids behave like salts. The presence of both acidic (carboxylic group) and basic (amino group) groups in the same molecule causes this behaviour. Whater soluble vitamin must be taken regularly because 'B' group vitamins and vitamin C are water soluble, they must be consumed on a regular basis because they are eliminated in urine and cannot be kept (except for Vit 12). Because hydrogen bonds occur between certain pairs of bases, the two strands of DNA are complementary to each other. Adenine and thymine form hydrogen bonds, but cytosine and guanine form hydrogen bonds.
solution: Penta-acetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine (-OHNH 2 ) because there is no free -CHO group present. Instead of being simple amines or carboxylic acids, amino acids behave like salts. The presence of both acidic (carboxylic group) and basic (amino group) groups in the same molecule causes this behaviour. Whater soluble vitamin must be taken regularly because 'B' group vitamins and vitamin C are water soluble, they must be consumed on a regular basis because they are eliminated in urine and cannot be kept (except for Vit 12). Because hydrogen bonds occur between certain pairs of bases, the two strands of DNA are complementary to each other. Adenine and thymine form hydrogen bonds, but cytosine and guanine form hydrogen bonds.
solution_html:
1. Penta-acetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine (-OHNH2) because there is no free -CHO group present.
  2. Instead of being simple amines or carboxylic acids, amino acids behave like salts. The presence of both acidic (carboxylic group) and basic (amino group) groups in the same molecule causes this behaviour.
  3. Whater soluble vitamin must be taken regularly because 'B' group vitamins and vitamin C are water soluble, they must be consumed on a regular basis because they are eliminated in urine and cannot be kept (except for Vit 12).
  4. Because hydrogen bonds occur between certain pairs of bases, the two strands of DNA are complementary to each other. Adenine and thymine form hydrogen bonds, but cytosine and guanine form hydrogen bonds.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Carbohydrates are the major components of all living organisms. Sugars are carbohydrates. The major types of sugars include monosaccharides and disaccharides. The main difference between monosaccharides, disaccharides and polysaccharides is that monosaccharides are monomer of sugars and disaccharides are composed of two monomers, whereas polysaccharides are composed of a large number of monomers. Monosaccharides are single sugar molecules which act as the building blocks of disaccharides and polysaccharides. Disaccharides are also simple sugars. Disaccharides are classified into two groups according to their reducing strength : Reducing and Non-reducing sugars. When a polymer is formed from a monomer, a condensation reaction occurs that forms a glycosidic bond and water molecule is lost. Starch, glycogen and cellulose are examples of polysaccharides. Starch is found in many parts of plant cell and consists of amylose and amylopectin. Glycogen is the major carbohydrate storage product found in humans. It is present in liver, muscles and brain.
Cellulose is the most abundant organic molecule on Earth. It makes up around 50% of all organic carbon.

  1. Name the linkage which connects monosaccharide units in polysaccharides. (1)
  2. Carbohydrates are classified on the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis. Write the hydrolysis products of sucrose. (1)
  3. Write two differences between Amylose and Amylopectin. (2)
    OR
    1. What are reducing sugars? (2)
    2. Sucrose is dextrorotatory but the mixture obtained after hydrolysis is laevorotatory. Why?
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Carbohydrates are the major components of all living organisms. Sugars are carbohydrates. The major types of sugars include monosaccharides and disaccharides. The main difference between monosaccharides, disaccharides and polysaccharides is that monosaccharides are monomer of sugars and disaccharides are composed of two monomers, whereas polysaccharides are composed of a large number of monomers. Monosaccharides are single sugar molecules which act as the building blocks of disaccharides and polysaccharides. Disaccharides are also simple sugars. Disaccharides are classified into two groups according to their reducing strength : Reducing and Non-reducing sugars. When a polymer is formed from a monomer, a condensation reaction occurs that forms a glycosidic bond and water molecule is lost. Starch, glycogen and cellulose are examples of polysaccharides. Starch is found in many parts of plant cell and consists of amylose and amylopectin. Glycogen is the major carbohydrate storage product found in humans. It is present in liver, muscles and brain. Cellulose is the most abundant organic molecule on Earth. It makes up around 50% of all organic carbon. Name the linkage which connects monosaccharide units in polysaccharides. (1) Carbohydrates are classified on the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis. Write the hydrolysis products of sucrose. (1) Write two differences between Amylose and Amylopectin. (2) OR What are reducing sugars? (2) Sucrose is dextrorotatory but the mixture obtained after hydrolysis is laevorotatory. Why?
explanation: Glycosidic linkage D-(+)-Glucose and D-(-)-fructose Amylose Amylopectin Soluble in water Insoluble in water Linear long chain polymer Branched chain polymer OR Sugars which reduce Fehling's / Tollen's reagents. Hydrolysis of sucrose gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. But the laevorotation of fructose is more than the dextrorotation of glucose. Hence, the mixture is laevorotatory.
solution: Glycosidic linkage D-(+)-Glucose and D-(-)-fructose Amylose Amylopectin Soluble in water Insoluble in water Linear long chain polymer Branched chain polymer OR Sugars which reduce Fehling's / Tollen's reagents. Hydrolysis of sucrose gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. But the laevorotation of fructose is more than the dextrorotation of glucose. Hence, the mixture is laevorotatory.

1. Glycosidic linkage
  2. D-(+)-Glucose and D-(-)-fructose
  3. Amylose Amylopectin
    Soluble in water Insoluble in water
    Linear long chain polymer Branched chain polymer
    OR
    1. Sugars which reduce Fehling's / Tollen's reagents.
    2. Hydrolysis of sucrose gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. But the laevorotation of fructose is more than the dextrorotation of glucose. Hence, the mixture is laevorotatory.

question_html:
Give a plausible explanation for the following: 

  1. Glucose doesn't give a 2,4-DNP test.
  2. The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary.
  3. Starch and cellulose both contain glucose unit as monomer, yet they are structurally different.
question_text: Give a plausible explanation for the following: Glucose doesn't give a 2,4-DNP test. The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Starch and cellulose both contain glucose unit as monomer, yet they are structurally different.
explanation: Glucose does not have open chain structure and hence it does not have a free -CHO group. Actually the -CHO group combines with C 5 -OH to form a hemiacetal. Glucose largely exists in the cyclic hemiacetal form along with a very small amount (0.5 %) of the open-chain form. Since the concentration of the open-chain form is low and its reaction with 2,4-DNP is reversible, therefore, the formation of 2,4- DNP derivative cannot disturb the equilibrium to generate more of the open-chain form from the cyclic hemiacetal form and hence it does not react with 2,4- DNP. In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. As hydrogen bonds are formed between a specific pair of bases. Cytosine forms a hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms a hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other. Starch and cellulose are two very similar polymers. Starch is formed by C 1 -C 4 alpha glycosidic linkage, whereas cellulose is formed by C 1 -C 4 beta glycosidic linkage.
solution: Glucose does not have open chain structure and hence it does not have a free -CHO group. Actually the -CHO group combines with C 5 -OH to form a hemiacetal. Glucose largely exists in the cyclic hemiacetal form along with a very small amount (0.5 %) of the open-chain form. Since the concentration of the open-chain form is low and its reaction with 2,4-DNP is reversible, therefore, the formation of 2,4- DNP derivative cannot disturb the equilibrium to generate more of the open-chain form from the cyclic hemiacetal form and hence it does not react with 2,4- DNP. In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. As hydrogen bonds are formed between a specific pair of bases. Cytosine forms a hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms a hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other. Starch and cellulose are two very similar polymers. Starch is formed by C 1 -C 4 alpha glycosidic linkage, whereas cellulose is formed by C 1 -C 4 beta glycosidic linkage.
solution_html:
1. Glucose does not have open chain structure and hence it does not have a free -CHO
    group. Actually the -CHO group combines with C5-OH to form a hemiacetal.
    Glucose largely exists in the cyclic hemiacetal form along with a very small amount (0.5 %) of the open-chain form. Since the concentration of the open-chain form is low and its reaction with 2,4-DNP is reversible, therefore, the formation of 2,4- DNP derivative cannot disturb the equilibrium to generate more of the open-chain form from the cyclic hemiacetal form and hence it does not react with 2,4- DNP.
  2. In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. As hydrogen bonds are formed between a specific pair of bases. Cytosine forms a hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms a hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.
  3. Starch and cellulose are two very similar polymers. Starch is formed by C1-C4 alpha glycosidic linkage, whereas cellulose is formed by C1-C4 beta glycosidic linkage.

question_text: How are vitamins classified? Name the vitamin responsible for the coagulation of blood.
explanation: On the basis of their solubility in water or fat, vitamins are classified into two groups. Fat-soluble vitamins: Vitamins that are soluble in fat and oils, but not in water, belong to this group. For example: Vitamins A, D, E, and K. Water-soluble vitamins: Vitamins that are soluble in water belong to this group. For example: B group vitamins ( {tex}{{\rm{B}}_1},{{\rm{B}}_2},{{\rm{B}}_6},{{\rm{B}}_{12}}{/tex} , etc.) and vitamin C However, biotin or vitamin H is neither soluble in water nor in fat. Vitamin K is responsible for the coagulation of blood.

The form (m.p. = 419 K) crystallises from a concentrated solution of glucose at 303 K and the {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} form (m.p = 423 K) crystallises from a hot and saturated aqueous solution at 371 K. This behavior cannot be explained by the open chain structure of glucose.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What are enzymes?
question_text: What are enzymes?
explanation: Enzymes are proteins that catalyse biological reactions. They are very specific in nature and catalyse only a particular reaction for a particular substrate. Enzymes are usually named after the particular substrate or class of substrate and sometimes after the particular reaction. For example, the enzyme used to catalyse the hydrolysis of maltose into glucose is named as maltase. {tex}\mathop {{C_{12}}{H_{22}}{O_{11}}}\limits_{Maltose} \xrightarrow[]{Maltase}\mathop {2{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}}\limits_{Glu\cos e} {/tex} Again, the enzymes used to catalyse the oxidation of one substrate with the simultaneous reduction of another substrate are named as oxidoreductase enzymes. The name of an enzyme ends with {tex}- {\rm{ase}}'{/tex} .

question_html: Write the important structural and functional differences between DNA and RNA.
question_text: Write the important structural and functional differences between DNA and RNA.
explanation: The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose. 1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose. 2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U). 2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T). 3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded. 3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded. The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity. 1. RNA is not responsible for heredity. 2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells. 2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.

The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose. 2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U). 2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T). 3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded. 3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded. The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity. 1. RNA is not responsible for heredity. 2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells. 2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.
solution_html:
The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows:

DNA

RNA

1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose.

1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose.

2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U).

2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T).

3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded.

3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded.


The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows:

DNA

RNA

1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity.

1. RNA is not responsible for heredity.

2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells.

2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.

question_html: What is glycogen? How is it different from starch? How is starch structurally different from cellulose?
question_text: What is glycogen? How is it different from starch? How is starch structurally different from cellulose?
explanation: Glycogen is a polysaccharide carbohydrate which is stored in animal body. It is also known as animal starch. Starch is a polymer of a-glucose and consists of two components amylose and amylopectin. Amylose is a linear polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-glucose. Both glycogen and amylopectin are branched polymers of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-glucose but glycogen is more highly branched than amylopectin. Amylopectin consists of 20-25 glucose units while glycogen chain consists of 10-14 glucose units. Starch is a branched chain polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-glucose, while cellulose is a linear polymer of {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose. Cellulose is present in the cell wall of plants while starch is the storage material of plants.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Describe what do you understand by primary and secondary structure of proteins?
question_text: Describe what do you understand by primary and secondary structure of proteins?
explanation: Primary structure In a protein molecule, one or more polypeptide chains may be present. Each polypeptide chain in a protein is linked together in a specific sequence of amino acids, this sequence of amino acids is termed as primary structure of proteins. Any change in primary structure, i.e. in the sequence of amino acid generates a new protein. Secondary structure It refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. They are found to exist in two different type of structures, a-helix and 13-pleatedsheet structure. These structures arise due to regular folding of the backbone of polypeptide chain due to hydrogen bonding between {tex} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - {/tex} and -NH- groups of the peptide bond.
solution: Primary structure In a protein molecule, one or more polypeptide chains may be present. Each polypeptide chain in a protein is linked together in a specific sequence of amino acids, this sequence of amino acids is termed as primary structure of proteins. Any change in primary structure, i.e. in the sequence of amino acid generates a new protein. Secondary structure It refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. They are found to exist in two different type of structures, a-helix and 13-pleatedsheet structure. These structures arise due to regular folding of the backbone of polypeptide chain due to hydrogen bonding between {tex} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - {/tex} and -NH- groups of the peptide bond.
solution_html:
1. Primary structure In a protein molecule, one or more polypeptide chains may be present. Each polypeptide chain in a protein is linked together in a specific sequence of amino acids, this sequence of amino acids is termed as primary structure of proteins. Any change in primary structure, i.e. in the sequence of amino acid generates a new protein.
  2. Secondary structure It refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. They are found to exist in two different type of structures, a-helix and 13-pleatedsheet structure. These structures arise due to regular folding of the backbone of polypeptide chain due to hydrogen bonding between {tex} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - {/tex} and -NH- groups of the peptide bond.

question_html: The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Explain.
question_text: The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Explain.
explanation: In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. Cytosine forms hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.
